reasoning: Line 26 of index.js does copy opts.headers values verbatim into the outgoing headers object without any CRLF sanitization — that code pattern is factually present. However, the claimed vulnerability (header injection / HTTP request splitting) is not exploitable in any currently supported Node.js runtime. Node.js enforces strict header value validation at the http/https module level via llhttp: passing a header value containing \r or \n throws 'Invalid character in header content' synchronously before any bytes are transmitted, regardless of whether headers are supplied as an object to http.request() options or via setHeader(). This was confirmed by direct testing on Node.js v22.22.2 (llhttp 9.3.0). The finding's own qualifier — 'in runtimes that do not enforce HTTP/1.1 header field rules' — acknowledges the dependency on a non-default runtime, but simple-get v4.0.1 uses require('http') directly and targets Node.js environments where this protection is active. There is no schema validation inside simple-get itself, but there is no sanitizer needed because the platform-level guard is present and sufficient. The finding describes a theoretical code smell, not a reachable vulnerability in any Node.js version still receiving security updates (v18, v20, v22).
